event: Archaic-period peoples, among the earliest known inhabitants of the Caribbean, settle Trinidad as evidenced by sites such as Banwari Trace.
Saladoid Arawak-speaking agriculturalists migrate from South America, introducing pottery, cassava cultivation, and village life to Trinidad.
Distinct indigenous groups including the Nepoya, Suppoya, and Yao live in Trinidad, with Carib-speaking peoples also present in the region.
Christopher Columbus reaches Trinidad on his third voyage on 31 July, naming the island and sighting Tobago from a distance.
Spain attempts early colonization of Trinidad, but settlement efforts repeatedly fail amid indigenous resistance.
Domingo de Vera founds San Jose de Oruna (Saint Joseph), the first permanent Spanish town and capital of Trinidad.
English captain Sir Walter Raleigh attacks and burns Saint Joseph while exploring the Orinoco region in search of El Dorado.
Dutch settlers establish a colony on Tobago, beginning a long period of contested European control over the island.
Capuchin missionaries begin establishing Catholic missions among Trinidad's indigenous population under Spanish rule.
Amerindians at the Arena mission revolt and kill Spanish priests and the governor, after which the rebellion is brutally suppressed.
The Spanish Crown issues the Cedula of Population, inviting Catholic settlers and their enslaved Africans, transforming Trinidad's economy and demographics.
A British expedition under Sir Ralph Abercromby captures Trinidad from Spain, which surrenders without major resistance.
The Treaty of Amiens formally cedes Trinidad to Britain, making it a British colony.
Britain abolishes the transatlantic slave trade, curbing the importation of enslaved Africans into Trinidad.
Tobago is ceded to Britain by France under the Treaty of Paris, ending decades of shifting European control.
Slavery is abolished throughout the British Empire, with an apprenticeship system imposed on the formerly enslaved in Trinidad and Tobago.
Full emancipation takes effect as the apprenticeship system ends, freeing the remaining enslaved population.
The first indentured laborers from India arrive aboard the Fatel Razack to work Trinidad's sugar plantations.
Tobago becomes part of the Windward Islands colonial administration as its sugar economy declines.
Trinidad and Tobago are administratively united into a single British Crown Colony.
The Water Riots erupt in Port of Spain over water-rate restrictions, ending with the burning of the Red House.
Indian indentured immigration to Trinidad is ended after decades that reshaped the colony's population and culture.
A major dockworkers' strike and labor unrest spread across Trinidad in the aftermath of World War I.
Widespread labor riots led by oilfield workers under Tubal Uriah Butler shake the colony and spur the rise of trade unions.
The United States establishes military bases at Chaguaramas and elsewhere under a wartime lease arrangement with Britain.
Eric Williams founds the People's National Movement, which goes on to dominate Trinidad and Tobago's politics.
Trinidad and Tobago joins the West Indies Federation, with Port of Spain serving as the federal capital.
Trinidad and Tobago gains independence from Britain on 31 August with Eric Williams as the first prime minister, and joins the United Nations.
The Black Power movement and a mutiny within the Defence Force lead to a state of emergency as social tensions peak.
Trinidad and Tobago becomes a republic, replacing the British monarch with a president as head of state.
Prime Minister Eric Williams dies in office after nearly 25 years leading the nation.
The National Alliance for Reconstruction wins elections, ending three decades of unbroken People's National Movement rule.
Members of the Jamaat al Muslimeen stage a violent coup attempt, holding the prime minister and parliament hostage for six days.
Basdeo Panday becomes the country's first prime minister of Indian descent after the United National Congress wins power.
Tobago gains expanded self-government autonomy through reforms to the Tobago House of Assembly.
Trinidad and Tobago co-hosts the Cricket World Cup, showcasing its sporting and cultural prominence in the Caribbean.
Kamla Persad-Bissessar becomes the nation's first female prime minister at the head of a People's Partnership coalition.
The People's National Movement under Keith Rowley returns to power in general elections.
Paula-Mae Weekes is inaugurated as the country's first female president.
The COVID-19 pandemic prompts border closures and public-health measures across Trinidad and Tobago.
Kamla Persad-Bissessar returns as prime minister after the United National Congress wins general elections.
